FULL CONTENT:
# Digital piracy is defined as the unauthorized use of another's work

![Digital piracy is defined as the unauthorized use of another's work](https://www.muso.com/hubfs/digital-piracy-unauthorized-use-resized.jpg)

## Digital piracy is defined as the unauthorized use or reproduction of another's work.

A pirate is defined as a person who appropriates or reproduces the work of another **for profit** without permission, usually in contravention of a patent or copyright.

A whole generation of people has grown up visiting unlicensed websites and delivery methods to view and consume digital content and this generation has been labelled as pirates. The ‘pirate’ audience is not, largely, in it for profit and it’s time we redefine the audience as an ‘unlicensed’ audience.

The unlicensed audience wants to access the content when they want it.

There are many drivers at play here; price sensitivity, windowing, licensing restrictions or platform accessibility. In an increasingly competitive SVoD market, this unlicensed audience will inevitably continue to seek out alternative methods of accessing content via unlicensed sites.

The subtlety of semantics here means that by defining this audience as pirates we run the risk of ignoring them as unreachable, beyond rehabilitation, but as I have long argued this audience are consumer; they buy shoes, have bank accounts, subscribe to some platforms but not all and are high intent fans but not in it for profit.

This audience, if embraced, can provide rights-holders, content creators and content platforms a valuable resource of data and insight. Geographical consumption trends, for example; engagement and popularity, genre trends, title comparisons, new releases and back catalogue trends amongst many others.

And this is a vast audience. Across 2018 MUSO tracked 189bn visits to unlicensed sites - or put another way, that’s 56.4 visits per internet user. If we continue to refer to this as a pirate audience we run the risk of marginalising and ignoring it, hoping it will go away. If we embrace it as an unlicensed audience then we have a significant opportunity to understand, convert and monetise. Nuance is everything.

[Discover our Protect solution to learn about digital piracy protection.](/products/protect)

##### [Article](https://www.muso.com/magazine/tag/article)

![](https://px.ads.linkedin.com/collect/?pid=4733970&fmt=gif)

DESCRIPTION: The image shows a infographic detailing the breakdown of digital media piracy, with percentages for categories such as television (24%), adult content (23%), magazines/e-books (14%), music (12%), movies (14%), and video (12%), based on pirated assets seized between January 2012 and March 2015.

IMAGE 2:
URL: https://gumletblog.gumlet.io/learn/2023/12/Video-Piracy-Statistics.png
DESCRIPTION: The content outlines key statistics related to video piracy, indicating that pirated material gains over 230 billion views annually, with over 80% of global online piracy linked to illegal streaming services, resulting in significant economic losses and job impacts in the US.

IMAGE 3:
URL: https://vpnalert.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/03/top-6-piracy-statistics-768x641.png
DESCRIPTION: The content highlights key piracy statistics, including 82 billion visits to piracy websites in 2021, 52.3% of users preferring to stream pirated content, and significant engagement with pirated music and the game Minecraft, alongside the generation of $1.3 billion in annual revenue from these sites.

IMAGE 4:

enriched_points.expanded_content: Digital piracy is the unauthorized reproduction, distribution, or sharing of digital content, encompassing music, films, software, and ebooks without the copyright owner's permission.
In 2021, there were 82 billion visits to piracy websites globally, indicating a widespread engagement with unlicensed content across various digital platforms.
The Malaysian media industry loses approximately RM3 billion annually due to digital piracy, showcasing significant financial repercussions for creators and the economy.
Digital piracy adversely affects industries like film, music, and software, resulting in lost revenue, job reductions, and stifled innovation within the creative sectors.
Enforcing copyright laws against digital piracy is challenging due to the anonymity of online users and the global nature of the internet, complicating legal actions.
